column1: GHANA FUEL PRICE — DATA EXPLORATION & DESCRIPTIVE STATISTICS

column2: Fuel price in GHS jumped from GHS 0.0001/L (1990) to GHS 15/L (2025) — a 150,000× increase, almost entirely driven by cedi depreciation and inflation.

column1: 2. EXCHANGE RATE IS THE DOMINANT DRIVER
column2: Exchange rate rose from 0.033 GHS/USD (1990) to ~14.18 GHS/USD (2024) — a 430× depreciation of the cedi, directly amplifying all imported oil costs.
In USD terms, fuel remained in the $0.003–$1.25/L range, showing global market pricing. The GHS price surge is a local currency phenomenon.
Crude oil price (global benchmark) correlates with GHS fuel prices (r ? 0.89 with exchange rate interaction), but exchange rate dominates after 2013.
1995 (59.5%), 2000 (40.2%), 2001 (41.5%), 2022 (31.3%), 2023 (38.1%) are hyperinflationary outlier years that spiked fuel costs.
In 2007, GHS fuel price jumps from ~0.095 to ~0.98 GHS/L — a 10× jump. This reflects Ghana's currency redenomination (1 new GHS = 10,000 old cedis) in July 2007.
GDP growth collapsed to 0.51% in 2020; fuel price dipped slightly (4.9 GHS from 5.2). Global crude also fell to $38.58/bbl.
Ghana's debt crisis caused massive cedi depreciation (8.27 GHS/USD in 2022, 11.02 in 2023), driving fuel prices to 9.8 then 13.5 GHS/L.
